page.content: The following table presents a comparative analysis of data extracted from the CINAHL database, focusing on the impact of different dosing regimens on treatment outcomes. Specifically, the data delineates the responses associated with non-continuous and continuous dosing, with sample sizes of 11 and 28 respectively, culminating in a total of 39 records analyzed. The independent variable under scrutiny is the status at study entry, categorized into stable disease (SD) and progressive disease (PD). The standardized coefficient (β) is reported as 5.079, indicating a robust association between the dosing regimen and treatment response, while the standard error of 0.386 underscores the precision of this estimate. Further, the t-value of 13.16 and a p-value of less than .0001 suggest that the findings are statistically significant, reinforcing the relevance of the dosing strategy in influencing patient outcomes. This analysis provides critical insights into how dosing frequency may affect treatment efficacy, thereby informing clinical decision-making and future research directions.
